fund.name: HDFC Flexi Cap Fund - Growth Option - Direct Plan

allocations.rationale: This flexi-cap fund is our core ‘all-weather’ equity fund. Since you are in the new tax regime, we avoid extra-80C locks. A flexi-cap fund lets the manager move between large, mid, and small caps based on market conditions, giving us the growth you want with less stress than picking one cap yourself.

fund.name: ICICI Prudential Large & Mid Cap Fund - Direct Plan - Growth

allocations.rationale: We add a dedicated large-and-mid cap fund because your profile allows up to 40% in mid/small. This fund gives you pure exposure to bigger companies (the backbone of the market) and well-established mid-size firms. It is slightly less volatile than pure mid-cap funds while still growing faster than large-cap alone.

fund.name: Motilal Oswal Nifty Midcap 150 Index Fund - Direct Plan

allocations.rationale: This is the second half of your ‘mid-cap’ allowance (aiming for ~24% total mid-cap). Instead of a typical active mid-cap fund, we chose this low-fee index fund. It simply tracks the Nifty Midcap 150 index (the 150 best mid-size companies). It has very low yearly fees (0.20%) and historically delivers strong returns, letting you capture the full upside of mid-caps.

fund.name: Motilal Oswal BSE Enhanced Value Index Fund-Direct plan

allocations.rationale: This ‘Enhanced Value’ index fund is a special ‘smart’ bet. It picks undervalued large-cap stocks (companies trading below their real worth) and tracks a custom index. This strategy has historically outperformed the standard Nifty 50 over 3-5 years. It is an active index fund (low fees like an index fund, but with a value tilt that many experts like).

plan.setup_phase:
Step 1: Open a direct mutual fund account on an app like Groww, Kuvera, or Coin. You only need an Aadhaar, PAN, and a bank account linked to UPI.

Step 2: Use your app to set up a monthly SIP in each of the four funds above. Enter the amounts shown (₹35,020, ₹14,420, ₹10,300, ₹6,180). Your account can easily handle this.

Step 3: For your existing corpus of ₹24.4L (excluding the medical corpus), use ₹5.96L (24% of corpus) to invest as a lump sum in these same four funds in their current allocation. Do this over 1–2 months (not all at once) to reduce the risk of buying at a peak. This is called ‘phased lumpsum’. 

Step 4: Use the first ₹50,000 of your ₹10,300 monthly surplus to build your ‘medical emergency corpus’ in a liquid or ultra-short-term debt fund (see the squad decision below). For simplicity, we will keep the entire debt part in one safe fund,
review_checkpoints:
After 6 months: Log in and confirm all SIPs are running. Did the money auto-debit? 

After 1 year: Compare your four funds’ performance with their respective category benchmarks (or peers) on a free app like ET Mutual Fund or Value Research. If one fund is trailing its peers by 5%+ annually, consider switching to another fund.

After 3-5 years: Review your allocation percentages. If equity grew very fast and is now >60% of your portfolio, you may gently rebalance (pull some money from equity and put it into your safety fund). 

Every year before New Year: Think about whether your ₹10,300 monthly surplus has grown with your salary. If yes, consider raising your SIPs by 8–10% to keep pace with inflation.

Once a year (preferably before New Year), check your total equity vs debt allocation. Equity includes flexi, mid, intelligent-cap, and arbitrage; debt includes your safety fund.

If equity is now 15–20% above your target (e.g., 52% vs 40%), sell enough equity to bring it back. The money goes straight into your debt fund. If equity is below target, move some debt money into equity. 

Why? Stocks rise faster but also fall harder. By keeping a disciplined 60–40 split, you protect yourself when markets drop 20–30%.

At age 55, you are in your peak earning years. You have 10 years ahead until retirement. This is long enough in Indian markets to ride out a bad year or two and still reap strong returns. We kept ~50% in mid/small and ‘intelligent’ equity to keep pace with inflation, because ₹10 lakh in 10 years will have much less buying power than today. We also kept ~30% in safer options so that if the stock market drops 30% one year (it often does), you are not left without any breathing room for your medical emergency.

Stock markets can drop 30% in a bad year — your portfolio value will temporarily go down. This is normal and usually recovers over 3–5 years. To handle this, keep your medical emergency money in a safe debt fund, and enjoy your corpus only after age 65.

Mid and small-cap funds can be 2–3 times more volatile than large caps. You can see them drop 40–50% in a severe cycle (like 2008 or 2020). This is why we also included a low-fee, valuation-based fund that historically has been less wild.

Gold funds are slab-taxed. Though gold protects portfolios against inflation and crises, gains over 10 years are taxed at your marginal slab (which is currently 0% for savings above a threshold). For post-retirement stability, we avoid gold here, but if you still want exposure, you can add a small slice in a gold ETF like ICICI Prudential Gold ETF.
This is for educational purposes only. Not certified financial advice.
Increase your total monthly SIP by 8% (about ₹8,240 more per year) to match salary growth and beat inflation over the next 10 years.

This 50% ‘attack’ and 50% ‘defence’ split suits a moderate investor at 55 with 10 years to go. The plan leverages your 40% mid/small-cap allowance, uses low-fee options, and keeps tax drag minimal since you are in the new regime. The ‘Enhanced Value’ fund and index funds provide high-return exposure without overpaying in fees.
plan.disclaimer: For research/educational purposes only. Not certified financial advice.

allocation_schedule.trigger: Final years: keep most growth, reduce volatility with atm style factors

aps.reasoning: The plan is heavily weighted toward passive and semi-passive strategies (Rotational 25% + Index Funds 60% + Intelligent Selection 15%), resulting in a low passive_instrument_fraction. The strategy emphasizes low fees and valuation factors (Avoids high expense, uses Enhanced Value index), supporting high cost and research scores. The 10-year horizon is consistent, though the advice to rebalance annually slightly lowers the time horizon alignment score. While the strategy uses rule-based factors rather than deep stock picking, the inclusion of active flexi/mid-cap funds gives a moderate portfolio_activeness_score.

pqs.reasoning: The plan shows strong strengths in tax efficiency (leveraging the new regime) and diversification (multiple caps and a value tilt) but has a significant gap regarding the investor's specific goal of building a 'Medical emergency corpus of 30L'. While the plan mentions using ₹50k monthly for this, it stops there without detailing how the *corpus itself* should be deployed for liquidity or when that phase is complete. Furthermore, the 'allocation_schedule' table for Year 10 contradicts the text which says to 'reduce volatility', yet the table shows equity rising to 55% (higher risk), whereas the text aims for stability in final years. This contradiction lowers internal consistency. The risk-return profile is appropriate for a moderate investor with 10 years left, but the goal alignment is slightly dinged by the unaddressed medical corpus deployment strategy.
